perturbation.original_text: 1. As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. As used herein, the term “Proprietary Data” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Proprietary Information" to "Proprietary Data" in its definition creates a contradiction. The rest of the contract refers to "Proprietary Information," leaving the scope of "Proprietary Data" unclear.  The contract now uses different terminologies to define the confidential nature of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Proprietary Information,' which the contradicted text then relies upon by stating 'The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence.' The changed text redefines 'Proprietary Information' as 'Proprietary Data.' This creates a direct conflict because the Receiving Party's obligation now refers to 'Proprietary Information', which is not defined, while 'Proprietary Data', the defined term, is not referenced in the contradicted section. This causes a strong contradiction under Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Data In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Confidential Data (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Confidential Data or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Confidential Data except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Confidential Data. Any employee given access to any such Confidential Data must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Confidential Data practically identical to the terms set forth herein.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "Proprietary Information" with "Confidential Data" within this section without updating the definition in Section 1, introduces ambiguity. It is now unclear if "Confidential Data" falls under the definition of "Proprietary Information" or is a separate class of data. This creates an in-text contradiction. It does not define Confidential Data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential. Proprietary Information may include, for example and without limitation, the product offering, content partners, product pricing, product availability, computer programs, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, schematics, trade secrets, processes, formulas, data, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), techniques, marketing plans, forecasts and strategies, and information concerning customers or vendors. Proprietary Information shall also include all information of a like nature owned by any other person and furnished to the Disclosing Party by such other person pursuant to an undertaking by the Disclosing Party to maintain the same in confidence.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses "Proprietary Information" which is defined in the contradicted text. The changed text replaces all instances of "Proprietary Information" with "Confidential Data". Because "Confidential Data" is never defined, it introduces ambiguity, thus creating an in-text contradiction. Specifically, the contradicted section defines "Proprietary Information", and the change now references an undefined term, making it unclear if the obligations related to "Proprietary Information" still apply to "Confidential Data".

perturbation.original_text: 3. Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or three days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, destroy and certify to the Receiving party in writing the destruction of all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or three days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, destroy and certify to the Receiving party in writing the destruction of all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Material and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "Proprietary Information" to "Proprietary Material" creates confusion. The agreement is unclear whether these terms can be used interchangeably or not. This directly contradicts the consistent use and initial definition of "Proprietary Information" as the standard for confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential. Proprietary Information may include, for example and without limitation, the product offering, content partners, product pricing, product availability, computer programs, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, schematics, trade secrets, processes, formulas, data, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), techniques, marketing plans, forecasts and strategies, and information concerning customers or vendors. Proprietary Information shall also include all information of a like nature owned by any other person and furnished to the Disclosing Party by such other person pursuant to an undertaking by the Disclosing Party to maintain the same in confidence.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Proprietary Information" to "Proprietary Material" introduces a misaligned terminology. The original "Proprietary Information" is specifically defined in the "Contradicted Text" section. By introducing "Proprietary Material", the contract creates ambiguity about whether the two terms are interchangeable. This directly contradicts the defined term of "Proprietary Information" and creates potential confusion in the contract's interpretation. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this contradiction, highlighting the created confusion.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Client will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Receiving Party" to "Client" introduces a contradiction. Throughout the document, "Receiving Party" is used to denote the party receiving the confidential information. This change creates confusion whether "Client" is the same entity as "Receiving Party".
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each undersigned party (the “Receiving Party”) and their respective directors, officers, employees, agents, or advisors (including, without limitation, legal advisors, accountants, consultants, bankers, and financial advisors) and potential sources of financing (collectively, “Representatives”) understands that during these discussions the other party (the “Disclosing Party’’) may disclose Proprietary Information (as defined herein) relating to the Disclosing Party’s business.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to the 'Receiving Party', which is consistently defined elsewhere in the document. The changed text replaces 'Receiving Party' with 'Client' in Section 5, creating a contradiction because the 'Receiving Party' is defined as the party receiving confidential information, and the introduction of 'Client' creates ambiguity as to whether the 'Client' and 'Receiving Party' are the same, which contradicts the definition of Receiving party found within the Contradicted Text, making it difficult to determine who is responsible for notifying the Disclosing Party of a breach.

perturbation.original_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Open Text Corporation will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Open Text Corporation (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Open Text Corporation has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Open Text Corporation on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Open Text Corporation.
perturbation.explanation: Interchanging "Company" with the explicit name "Open Text Corporation" creates an in-text contradiction because it introduces confusion over the consistency of referring to Open Text Corporation, the term Company should only be used. The consistent use of the abbreviation "Company" throughout the agreement is broken.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement (the “Agreement”) is made by and between Actuate Corporation, a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business located at 951 Mariners Island Boulevard, San Mateo, CA 94404 (“Actuate”), and Open Text Corporation, a corporation incorporated pursuant to the laws of Canada, with its principal place of business located at 275 Frank Tompa Drive, Waterloo, ON N2L OA1 (“Company”). Actuate and Company intend to discuss a potential business relationship. Each undersigned party (the “Receiving Party”) and their respective directors, officers, employees, agents, or advisors (including, without limitation, legal advisors, accountants, consultants, bankers, and financial advisors) and potential sources of financing (collectively, “Representatives”) understands that during these discussions the other party (the “Disclosing Party’’) may disclose Proprietary Information (as defined herein) relating to the Disclosing Party’s business.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'Company' which aligns with the definition provided in the 'Contradicted Text' where 'Company' is explicitly defined as 'Open Text Corporation.' The changed text replaces 'Company' with 'Open Text Corporation' in the non-solicitation clause. This creates an inconsistency because the agreement is designed to refer to Open Text Corporation as 'Company'. Therefore, the change introduces a 'Misaligned Terminology' that causes an in-text contradiction as explained in the 'Perturbation Explanation'.
